title: Introduction to Programming – Functional Programming Essentials Quiz

explanation.`Ranking of LLMs: IRIS = CHATGPT`: Correct. The method reference `String::toUpperCase` is assigned to `upperCase` as a lambda implementation of `StringOperation`. Calling `upperCase.manipulate("iris < ChatGPT")` converts the entire string to uppercase, yielding `"IRIS < CHATGPT"`. This result is then passed to `replaceChar.manipulate()`, which uses the lambda `(input) -> input.replace("<", "=")` to replace the `<` symbol with `=`, producing the final output `"IRIS = CHATGPT"`.
explanation.`Ranking of LLMs: iris = ChatGPT`: Incorrect. The method reference `String::toUpperCase` is applied first, converting the input to uppercase before the replacement step. The intermediate result is `"IRIS < CHATGPT"`, not the original lowercase string.
explanation.`Ranking of LLMs: IRIS < CHATGPT`: Incorrect. While `toUpperCase()` is correctly applied, this would be the output only if `replaceChar.manipulate()` were never called. The code explicitly passes `resultAfterToUpperCase` to `replaceChar.manipulate()`, which replaces `<` with `=`.
explanation.Compilation Error: Incorrect. The code is syntactically valid. `StringOperation` is a functional interface with a single abstract method `manipulate(String)`, so it can legally be implemented via a method reference (`String::toUpperCase`) or a lambda expression. No compilation error occurs.

questions.source_reference: W08 Functional Programming Essentials – Lambda expressions, method references (String::toUpperCase), functional interfaces

options: `The chat app SLACK is used by 500 users.`
`The chat app DISCORD is used by 500 users.`
`The chat app ZULIP is used by 1000 users.`
`The chat app SLACK is used by 250 users.`
questions.correct_answer: `The chat app SLACK is used by 500 users.`

explanation.`The chat app SLACK is used by 500 users: Correct. `chatApp` is assigned `ChatApplication.SLACK`. When concatenated in the print statement, an enum value's `toString()` returns its name, so `chatApp` prints as `SLACK`. The switch expression inside `getUserNumbers()` matches `case SLACK` and returns `500`.
explanation.`The chat app DISCORD is used by 500 use: Incorrect. `DISCORD` is not assigned to `chatApp`; the variable holds `ChatApplication.SLACK`. The switch expression would return `1000` for `DISCORD`, not `500`.
explanation.`The chat app ZULIP is used by 1000 user: Incorrect. `chatApp` is assigned `SLACK`, not `ZULIP`. Additionally, `getUserNumbers()` returns `250` for `ZULIP` and `1000` for `DISCORD` — neither matches this output.
explanation.`The chat app SLACK is used by 250 users: Incorrect. While `chatApp` is correctly identified as `SLACK`, the switch expression returns `500` for that case, not `250`. The value `250` corresponds to `ZULIP`.

questions.source_reference: W08 Functional Programming Essentials – Enum types, switch expressions (arrow syntax, no fall-through, exhaustiveness)

options: `The communication channel with the most votes has received 96 votes.`
`The communication channel with the most votes has received 58 votes.`
`The communication channel with the most votes has received 62 votes.`
`The communication channel with the most votes has received 43 votes.`
questions.correct_answer: `The communication channel with the most votes has received 96 votes.`

explanation.`The communication channel with the most: Correct. The anonymous inner class implements `Comparator<Integer>` with `compare(val1, val2)` returning `val2 - val1`. When `val2 > val1` the result is positive, so `val2` is placed before `val1` — this sorts the list in descending order. After sorting the list is `[96, 62, 58, 43]`, so `get(0)` returns `96`, the highest vote count.
Incorrect. The `Comparator` sorts the list in descending order (`val2 - val1`), placing the largest value at index 0. `58` ends up at index 2 after sorting, not at index 0.
Incorrect. After a descending sort the list is `[96, 62, 58, 43]`. `62` is at index 1, not index 0, so `get(0)` does not return it.

Incorrect. `43` would be at index 0 only if the comparator used `val1 - val2`, which sorts in ascending order. Because `val2 - val1` is used, the list is sorted in descending order and `43` ends up last.

questions.source_reference: W08 Functional Programming Essentials – Collections.sort(), Comparator<T> (anonymous inner class, compare() return value semantics, ascending vs descending order)

learning_objectives: Trace the execution of a functional interface implemented via a method reference and a lambda expression, predicting the string output after chained manipulations
Read an enum with a switch expression method and determine the printed output for a specific enum constant
Interpret a Comparator implemented as an anonymous inner class, identifying whether val2 - val1 produces ascending or descending order and predicting the element at a given list index after sorting
